---
title: "Legendre Polynomials"
bibliography: ../../../references.bib
---

← [Numerical Methods](../)

Source inspiration: [@mathewsSite].

## Animations

Each animation below illustrates Legendre polynomials $P_n(x)$ on $[-1,1]$. The first animation reveals $P_0$ through $P_5$ one per frame; the second shows the Legendre series partial sums converging to $f(x) = \cos(\pi x/2)$.

Julia source scripts that generated these animations are linked under each case.

### Case 1 — Successive polynomials $P_0, P_1, \ldots, P_5$

**Properties:** Each $P_n$ is orthogonal to all lower-degree Legendre polynomials on $[-1,1]$. $P_n(1)=1$, $P_n(-1)=(-1)^n$, and $P_n$ has exactly $n$ zeros in $(-1,1)$. They are computed by the recurrence $(n+1)P_{n+1} = (2n+1)xP_n - nP_{n-1}$.

[Julia source](legendrepolyaa.jl)

![Legendre polynomials P_0 through P_5 added one per frame on the interval [-1, 1], showing their oscillatory character](legendrepolyaa.gif)

### Case 2 — Legendre series for $f(x) = \cos(\pi x/2)$

**Behavior:** The partial sum $S_N(x) = \sum_{n=0}^{N} c_n P_n(x)$ with $c_n = \tfrac{2n+1}{2}\int_{-1}^{1} f(x)P_n(x)\,dx$ converges to $f$ as $N\to\infty$. Only even-$n$ terms contribute (since $f$ is even). Each added term refines the fit.

[Julia source](legendrepolybb.jl)

![Legendre series partial sums for cos(pi x/2) building up frame by frame, showing convergence to the true function](legendrepolybb.gif)

## Derivation Notes (Planned)

Short derivations will be added to explain the core equations and assumptions.
